explanation_md: Objects are constructed in the order they are declared: `a` first (prints `C1`), then `b` (prints `C2`). At the closing brace of `main`, they are destroyed in *reverse* order: `b` is destroyed first (prints `D2`), then `a` (prints `D1`). Destruction order is always the reverse of construction order.

prompt_md: When does the destructor of a local (stack) variable run?

options.a: When the program calls `delete` on the object
options.b: When the object goes out of scope — at the closing brace of the block where it was declared
options.d: Only if the constructor ran without throwing an exception

explanation_md: For stack (automatic) objects, the compiler inserts destructor calls automatically at the point where the variable goes out of scope — the closing `}` of the enclosing block. This is independent of `delete` (which is for heap objects), of whether `main` has finished, and of how the constructor completed.

prompt_md: A class `Widget` holds only an `int` and a `double` as data members. Should you write a custom destructor for it?

options.a: Yes — every class should have an explicit destructor for clarity
options.b: No — the compiler-generated destructor already destroys `int` and `double` members correctly
options.c: Yes — otherwise the `int` and `double` will not be zeroed out
options.d: No — destructors are only needed for classes with virtual functions

explanation_md: The compiler generates a destructor that destroys each data member in reverse order of declaration. For plain types like `int` and `double`, "destroying" them means nothing special. You only need a custom destructor when the class owns an external resource (file handle, raw pointer, etc.) that the compiler does not know how to release.

`r` is constructed when `run()` starts (prints `open`). Inside `run()`, `work` is printed. When `run()` returns, `r` goes out of scope and the destructor fires (prints `close`). Control returns to `main`, which prints `done`. The destructor runs before `run()` returns to its caller.

prompt_md: What is the primary danger of calling `obj.~MyClass()` explicitly on a stack object?

options.a: The destructor body runs twice: once explicitly and once when the object goes out of scope, potentially double-releasing a resource
options.b: The compiler will refuse to compile code that calls the destructor explicitly
options.c: The object's data members are zeroed out but the object remains in scope
options.d: It prevents the constructor from being called again on the same memory

explanation_md: Explicitly calling the destructor of a stack object does not prevent the compiler from also calling it when the object goes out of scope. For a class that frees a resource in its destructor, this means the resource is released twice — undefined behavior. Explicit destructor calls are only legitimate in advanced placement-new scenarios.
